════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 INTELLIGENCE BRIEFING — As of 2025-01-15
 Subject: Donald J. Trump
 Knowledge Window: 2025-01-01 → 2025-01-15
 Events in window: 28 | Showing top 8
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

[2025-01-08] Trump Informs Republican Senators of Plans for Over 100 Executive Orders Starting Day One
President-elect Donald Trump is preparing more than 100 executive orders, which he plans to launch starting on Day One of his new White House term, Inauguration Day, January 20, 2025. Trump informed Republican senators about the executive orders during a private meeting on Capitol Hill on January 8, 2025. Top adviser Stephen Miller outlined proposed border security and immigration enforcement measures. The planned executive actions are expected to cover a wide range of topics, including U.S.-Mexico border policies, energy development, federal workforce rules, school gender policies, and vaccine mandates. The new administration plans to rescind executive orders from the Biden administration and implement its own proposals, including completing the U.S.-Mexico border wall and establishing immigration detention facilities. An estimated $100 billion in funding is being sought for these initiatives. The administration also plans to revert to first-term border measures and deport an estimated 1 million migrants.
Key facts:
  • Estimated $100 billion in border proposals
  • Estimated 1 million migrants targeted for deportation
  • Meeting date: January 8, 2025
  • Over 100 executive orders planned
  • Planned launch date: Inauguration Day, January 20, 2025
  • Private meeting with Republican senators on Capitol Hill
  • Sen. James Lankford (R-Okla.)
  • Sen. John Hoeven (R-N.D.)
  • Senate Majority Leader John Thune
  • Stephen Miller (Trump top adviser)
"There will be a substantial number." — Sen. John Hoeven, R-N.D. (Commenting on the number of executive orders planned by Trump during a private meeting on Capitol Hill.)
"That’s the low-hanging fruit. People that recently crossed, people that were legally present and committed other crimes, people that the court has ordered them removed – that’s well over a million people. Start working through that process." — Sen. James Lankford, R-Okla. (Discussing the initial focus of Trump's deportation plans.)
"That would be nice. We’ll see." — Senate Majority Leader John Thune (Commenting on the possibility of Senate votes on Trump's nominees on Inauguration Day.)
Sources: apnews.com (1 source)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
[2025-01-03] House Speaker Mike Johnson Faces Vote to Retain Speakership in 119th Congress
House Speaker Mike Johnson is seeking re-election to the speakership for the 119th Congress, with the vote scheduled for Friday, January 3, 2025, at noon. Republicans hold a 219-215 majority, requiring Johnson to secure 218 votes if all members are present. Several Republican lawmakers, including Reps. Thomas Massie and Victoria Spartz, have expressed opposition, citing Johnson's deals on government spending, aid to Ukraine, and the renewal of FISA Section 702. President-elect Donald Trump publicly endorsed Johnson on December 30, 2024. Johnson met with several detractors, including members of the House Freedom Caucus, on January 2, 2025, to address their concerns. Johnson expressed confidence in retaining the gavel. Democrats are expected to vote for Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries. A proposed rules change for the new Congress would require nine majority party lawmakers to initiate a motion to vacate the speaker.
Key facts:
  • 119th Congress
  • FISA Section 702
  • House Freedom Caucus
  • House Freedom Caucus members present at meeting: Chairman Andy Harris (R-Md.), Andy Biggs (R-Ariz.), Scott Perry (R-Pa.), Ralph Norman (R-S.C.)
  • House Speaker Mike Johnson
  • House Speaker vote scheduled for January 3, 2025
  • Johnson met with detractors on January 2, 2025
  • Johnson needs 218 votes for simple majority
  • Mike Johnson (House Speaker)
  • Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.)
  • President-elect Donald Trump
  • President-elect Donald Trump's endorsement on December 30, 2024
  • Proposed rules change: 9 majority party lawmakers needed for motion to vacate
  • Proposed rules change: 9 majority party members needed for 'motion to vacate'
  • Rep. Thomas Massie (Kentucky)
  • Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.)
  • Rep. Victoria Spartz (Indiana)
  • Rep. Victoria Spartz (R-Ind.)
  • Republican House majority: 219-215
  • Republican majority: 219-215 in 119th Congress
  • Trump's endorsement on December 30, 2024
  • Vote for Speaker scheduled for January 3, 2025
  • Votes required for speaker: 218 (assuming all members present)
"I respect and support President Trump, but his endorsement of Mike Johnson is going to work out about as well as his endorsement of Speaker Paul Ryan. We’ve seen Johnson partner with the democrats to send money to Ukraine, authorize spying on Americans, and blow the budget." — Thomas Massie (On X before the new year)
"matters a whole lot" — Ralph Norman (Regarding Trump's endorsement, after meeting with Johnson on Thursday)
"people trust Mike Johnson." — Ralph Norman (After meeting with Johnson on Thursday)
"I hope so. That's the plan." — Mike Johnson (To reporters after meetings Thursday, regarding winning on the first ballot)
"We will get this done. Look, the things that we’re talking about this morning are an illustration that we live in very serious times. We cannot afford any palace drama here. We have got to get the Congress started, which begins tomorrow, and we have to get immediately to work." — Mike Johnson (On "Fox and Friends" earlier Thursday)
"We have to certify the election of President Donald J Trump on Jan. 6, on Monday. And we have many important things pressing on us right now, so there’s no time to waste. We have to stay unified." — Mike Johnson (On "Fox and Friends" earlier Thursday)
"We have unified government that begins tomorrow. We have the White House, the Senate and the House — a totally different situation than we dealt with over the last 14 months since I’ve been speaker. So we’re excited to deliver on the America First agenda." — Mike Johnson (On "Fox and Friends" earlier Thursday)
"We will get this done. Look, the things that we’re talking about this morning are an illustration that we live in very serious times. We cannot afford any palace drama here. We have got to get the Congress started, which begins tomorrow, and we have to get immediately to work. We have to certify the election of President Donald J Trump on Jan. 6, on Monday. And we have many important things pressing on us right now, so there’s no time to waste. We have to stay unified." — Mike Johnson (On "Fox and Friends" on Thursday, January 2, 2025)
Sources: www.yahoo.com, www.nbcnews.com (2 sources)

[2025-01-02] Incoming West Virginia Gov. Patrick Morrisey Announces Initial Staff and Cabinet Appointments
West Virginia Governor-elect Patrick Morrisey announced his initial cabinet appointments and executive team members during a press conference in Charleston on January 2, 2025. Morrisey named ten individuals to his executive team, including Tom McCaffery as Chief of Staff, Douglas P. Buffington II as Senior Advisor, and Crescent Gallagher as Press Secretary. Other executive appointments included A. Todd Johnston as Deputy Chief of Staff and Sean M. Whelan as General Counsel. Five individuals were appointed to cabinet roles, such as Senator Eric Nelson as Secretary of Revenue, Eric Householder as Secretary of Administration, and Alex Mayer as Secretary of Human Services. Harold Ward was retained as Secretary of the Department of Environmental Protection, and Dianna Graves was named Commissioner of the Bureau of Senior Services. Cabinet appointments are subject to approval by the legislative body.
Key facts:
  • A. Todd Johnston (Deputy Chief of Staff and Chair of Energy, Infrastructure, and Competitiveness Council)
  • A. Todd Johnston (Deputy Chief of Staff, Chair of Energy, Infrastructure, and Competitiveness Council)
  • Alex Mayer (Secretary of Human Services)
  • Announcement made in Charleston, West Virginia
  • Cabinet appointments subject to legislative approval
  • Crescent Gallagher (Press Secretary)
  • Curtis R. A. Capehart (Director of Policy)
  • Dianna Graves (Commissioner of Bureau of Senior Services)
  • Douglas P. Buffington II (Senior Advisor)
  • Eric Householder (Secretary of Administration)
  • Harold Ward (Secretary of the Department of Environmental Protection)
  • John L. Findlay (Director of Intergovernmental and Political Affairs)
  • Katie Franklin (Deputy General Counsel)
  • Marissa Findlay (Director of Operations)
  • Mike McKown (Director of Budget)
  • Patrick Morrisey (Governor-elect)
  • Patrick Morrisey (Incoming West Virginia Governor)
  • Sean M. Whelan (General Counsel)
  • Sen. Eric Nelson (Secretary of Revenue)
  • Senator Eric Nelson (Secretary of Revenue)
  • The Honorable Dianna Graves (Commissioner of Bureau of Senior Services)
  • The Honorable Eric Householder (Secretary of Administration)
  • Tom McCaffery (Chief of Staff)
"I think he’s a home run get for the state of West Virginia. I can’t imagine having someone with that kind of experience. He’s going to help the citizens of our state tremendously." — Patrick Morrisey (News conference, referring to Tom McCaffery's appointment as Chief of Staff)
"I’m really enjoying the working relationship with Sen. Smith. I think he’s going to do a terrific job. I’ve certainly been talking with the speaker as well. I anticipate a good, close working relationship. We have a lot of work to do in the upcoming months. It’s a thrilling time in West Virginia. I’m ready to make sure that everyone knows our team is second to none. That’s how we did really well in the AG’s office." — Patrick Morrisey (News conference, discussing working relationship with legislative body for cabinet approvals)
"I am grateful to each of these leaders for their willingness and desire to bring opportunity and prosperity to the Mountain State. We are united in our mission of promoting and protecting the freedoms and rights of every West Virginian. When we work together, West Virginia wins." — Patrick Morrisey (Announcing cabinet and executive team appointments)
Sources: wchstv.com, www.wdtv.com (2 sources)

[2025-01-14] Confirmation Hearings for Trump's Cabinet Nominees Begin
Confirmation hearings for President-elect Donald Trump's Cabinet nominees are scheduled to begin on Tuesday, January 14, 2025, and continue on Wednesday, January 15, 2025. The hearings allow nominees to answer questions regarding their qualifications and plans for managing government agencies. The Senate's advice and consent role requires all nominees to undergo public hearings with relevant committees, which will then vote on whether to recommend them to the full Senate. A simple majority is needed for confirmation in the Senate. The first hearing on Tuesday is for Defense Secretary nominee Pete Hegseth. Hearings on Wednesday include nominees for Attorney General Pam Bondi, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, and Homeland Security Kristi Noem. Other nominees, Tulsi Gabbard (Director of National Intelligence) and Kash Patel (FBI), have not yet had their hearings scheduled.
Key facts:
  • Attorney General nominee Pam Bondi
  • Defense Secretary nominee Pete Hegseth
  • Director of National Intelligence nominee Tulsi Gabbard
  • FBI nominee Kash Patel
  • Homeland Security nominee Kristi Noem
  • President-elect Donald Trump
  • Secretary of State nominee Marco Rubio
  • Senator Lisa Murkowski (Alaska)
  • Senator Susan Collins (Maine)
  • Senator Tammy Duckworth (Illinois Democrat)
  • Simple majority needed for Senate confirmation
Sources: www.npr.org (1 source)

[2025-01-10] Sentencing Audio Released in People v. Donald J. Trump Case
Audio of the sentencing in the criminal case of People v. Donald J. Trump was released on January 10, 2025. The release followed a Decision & Order issued on January 6, 2025, and other related filings by the prosecution and defense counsel Blanche Law on the same date. Court records detail a timeline of legal proceedings, including the filing of a verdict sheet on May 30, 2024, jury instructions on May 29, 2024, and multiple decisions and orders on motions related to contempt, discovery, immunity, and extrajudicial statements throughout 2024. Earlier filings included a joint application for adjournment on November 12, 2024, and various letters from Blanche Law and the District Attorney's office.
Key facts:
  • Audio of Sentencing provided: January 10, 2025
  • Blanche Law PLLC (defense counsel)
  • Case: People v. Donald J. Trump
  • Decision & Order issued: January 6, 2025
  • District Attorney (prosecution)
  • Judge Merchan
  • Jury Instructions & Charges filed: May 29, 2024
  • Verdict Sheet filed: May 30, 2024
Sources: ww2.nycourts.gov (1 source)

[2025-01-14] President Biden Signs Executive Order 14141 to Advance U.S. Leadership in AI Infrastructure
President Joseph R. Biden, Jr. signed Executive Order 14141, titled "Advancing United States Leadership in Artificial Intelligence Infrastructure," on January 14, 2025. The order aims to ensure that future frontier artificial intelligence (AI) is developed within the United States to bolster national security and economic competitiveness. It seeks to prevent adversaries from accessing powerful AI systems and reduce U.S. dependence on foreign AI infrastructure. The order mandates building domestic AI infrastructure, including advanced computing clusters and energy infrastructure, while promoting U.S. leadership in clean energy technologies like geothermal, solar, wind, and nuclear to power AI data centers. It outlines five guiding principles for executive departments and agencies, emphasizing national security, economic competitiveness, and clean power for AI development, consistent with previous directives such as National Security Memorandum 25 and Executive Order 14110.
Key facts:
  • Document Number: 2025-01395
  • Executive Order 14141
  • Outlines five guiding principles for AI infrastructure development
  • Published in the Federal Register as 90 FR 5469
  • References Executive Order 14110 (October 30, 2023)
  • References National Security Memorandum 25 (October 24, 2024)
  • Signed by President Joseph R. Biden, Jr.
Sources: www.federalregister.gov (1 source)

[2025-01-08] Trump Asks Supreme Court to Halt New York Hush Money Sentencing
President-elect Donald Trump filed an emergency request with the Supreme Court on January 8, 2025, asking the justices to halt his criminal sentencing scheduled for January 10, 2025, in his New York hush money case. The 40-page filing, signed by John Sauer, argued that preparing for sentencing while preparing to lead the country imposes an unconstitutional burden. Justice Sonia Sotomayor directed prosecutors to respond by January 9, 2025, at 10 a.m. Trump was found guilty in May of 34 counts of falsifying business records related to payments to adult film star Stormy Daniels. Judge Juan Merchan had previously rejected Trump's requests to delay or halt the sentencing on January 3 and January 6, 2025. A state appeals judge, Ellen Gesmer, also denied a similar request on January 7, 2025. Trump's appeal to the Supreme Court cited presidential immunity claims from *Trump v. United States*.
Key facts:
  • *Trump v. United States* (cited case)
  • 40-page filing
  • Adult film star Stormy Daniels
  • Donald Trump (President-elect)
  • Gesmer's denial on January 7, 2025
  • Guilty of 34 counts of falsifying business records
  • John Sauer (signer of filing, intended Solicitor General nominee)
  • Judge Juan Merchan
  • Justice Sonia Sotomayor
  • Merchan's opinion on January 3, 2025
  • Merchan's rejection of hold on January 6, 2025
  • New York hush money case
  • Prosecutors directed to respond by January 9, 2025, 10 a.m.
  • Sentencing scheduled for January 10, 2025
  • State appeals judge Ellen Gesmer
  • Supreme Court
  • Trump won 312 electoral votes
  • Trump won popular vote by approximately 1.5%
"Forcing President Trump to prepare for a criminal sentencing in a felony case while he is preparing to lead the free world as President of the United States in less than two weeks imposes an intolerable, unconstitutional burden on him that undermines these vital national interests" — Donald Trump (from filing) (Emergency filing to the Supreme Court)
"[A]ll that remains outstanding in this case is the issuance of this Decision and the imposition of sentence." — Judge Juan Merchan (Opinion on January 3, 2025, rejecting Trump's plea to delay sentencing)
Sources: www.scotusblog.com (1 source)

[2025-01-10] Donald Trump Sentenced to Unconditional Discharge in New York Hush Money Case
On Friday, January 10, 2025, Judge Juan Merchan sentenced President-elect Donald Trump to an unconditional discharge in the New York hush money case. Trump, speaking remotely from his Florida home, addressed the judge, stating that the case had been "a very terrible experience" and that he "did nothing wrong." The sentencing occurred days before Trump's presidential inauguration, scheduled for January 20.
Key facts:
  • Donald Trump
  • Judge Juan Merchan
  • New York hush money case
  • No-punishment unconditional discharge
  • Presidential inauguration on January 20
  • Sentencing on January 10, 2025
"a very terrible experience" — Donald Trump (Speaking remotely to Judge Merchan during sentencing)
"I did nothing wrong" — Donald Trump (Speaking remotely to Judge Merchan during sentencing)
Sources: www.yahoo.com (1 source)
